extracted_rules.resource: Right to safe harbor and maintenance
proof: [§1] Then he came to Creon and long-robed Eniocha, who received him kindly and gave him all fitting things, as is due to suppliants, and honored him in their hearts even more.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions that receiving a suppliant and providing 'all fitting things' is a 'due' (a social/legal obligation). This demonstrates that individuals seeking asylum or refuge held a recognized right to material support and protection in Theban society.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any person could potentially become a suppliant, it is a specific legal/social status distinct from the general population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to sexual access in marriage
proof: [§1] There he dwelt with his modest wife without the joys of love, nor might he go in unto the neat-ankled daughter of Electryon until he had avenged the death of his wife's great-hearted brothers and utterly burned with blazing fire the villages of the heroes, the Taphians and Teleboans; for this thing was laid upon him, and the gods were witnesses to it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a customary or semi-legal prohibition where a man who has caused family strife (killing the father-in-law) is barred from the 'joys of love' with his wife until he fulfills a specific blood-vengeance for her lineage.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific subset of married men dealing with blood-feuds or lineage-based obligations.

proof: [§1] And the men who were elders and on whom age had laid hold were all together outside the gates, and were holding up their hands to the blessed gods, fearing for their own sons.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The description on the shield reflects a societal norm where older men are stationed 'outside the gates' in a non-combatant role (prayer/supervision) while the younger men ('their own sons') perform the actual fighting.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all men of a certain age within the community.

proof: [§1] But the son of Alcmena and glorious Iolaus stripped the fine armour off Cycnus' shoulders and went, and their swift horses carried them straight to the city of Trachis.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Stripping the armor of a defeated foe is treated as a standard legal/customary right of the victor in Homeric/Hesiodic warfare, representing a significant transfer of material wealth.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a specific right reserved for active combatants in an aristocratic/warrior society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to lead public marriage festivals
proof: [§1] some were bringing home a bride to her husband on a well-wheeled car, while the bridal-song swelled high, and the glow of blazing torches held by handmaidens rolled in waves afar. And these maidens went before, delighting in the festival
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a structured social role where handmaidens and young women are legally/customarily tasked with leading the bridal procession, granting them specific visibility and participation in civic-religious life.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to young, unmarried women of specific social standing.
